nativeRange.habitat: The Clown Rasbora inhabits slow-moving to still blackwater streams, peat swamps, and forest-shaded pools. The water in these environments is heavily stained by dissolved tannins and humic acids leaching from leaf litter and submerged wood, with dense overhanging riparian vegetation blocking most direct light.

decorPreferences: dense planting with floating cover (Amazon frogbit, water lettuce, Indian almond leaves)
driftwood and twisted roots to create shaded midwater zones
dried leaf litter (Indian almond or oak leaves) on substrate to release tannins
dark or sandy substrate to lower contrast and reduce fish stress
peat filtration or blackwater extract to acidify and soften water
dim or diffused lighting with surface plant cover

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding on small insects, zooplankton, and invertebrates. In captivity readily accepts quality micro-pellets, crushed flake, and frozen or live foods such as daphnia, baby brine shrimp, micro-worms, and bloodworms. A varied diet with regular live or frozen food promotes best colour and condition.

goodWith: other small blackwater rasboras (e.g. lambchop rasbora, harlequin rasbora)
small peaceful cyprinids from Southeast Asia
chocolate gourami and other small blackwater gouramis
pygmy corydoras and small loaches tolerant of low pH
dwarf puffer (with caution — size match required)
small peaceful tetras tolerant of soft acidic water
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving species that may outcompete at feeding

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Factumquintus (German Wikipedia); 3504x2336. Sharp, well-lit aquarium shot of a vibrant adult showing the diagnostic rosy-pink body and both prominent black spots, with companion rasboras behind conveying the loosely shoaling habit. Best overall image.

Photo by Factumquintus; 1024x683. Same aquarium session as the recommended pick but a wider frame showing the individual plus tankmates; crisp, clearly shows the two black body spots on a rosy body. Lower resolution, near-duplicate of the primary.

summary: A large, boldly marked Southeast Asian rasbora from Sumatran and Bornean peat swamps, distinguished by two prominent black spots on a rosy-pink body. Requires warm, very soft, acidic blackwater conditions and is unsuitable for hard or alkaline setups. Seen less often in the trade than its striking appearance might suggest.
careNotes: The Clown Rasbora is one of the largest members of its genus, reaching 8–10 cm — considerably bigger than the common harlequin or lambchop rasbora. It originates from the peat swamp forests of Sumatra and Borneo (Peninsular Malaysia, Kalimantan, Sumatra, and Sarawak/Sabah), where water is dark with dissolved tannins and humic acids, extremely soft (gH near 0), and acidic (pH often 4.0–5.5). Replicating this blackwater chemistry is essential: fish kept in neutral or hard water tend to fade in colour, become stressed, and show increased susceptibility to disease. Peat filtration, reverse osmosis water treated with blackwater extract, or the addition of dried Indian almond leaves and oak leaves are all appropriate approaches. Temperature should be maintained between 23–28°C. A tank of at least 120 L with a 100 cm footprint is recommended for a group. Subdued lighting, floating plants, and driftwood help create the shaded, tannin-stained environment the species is adapted to. The Clown Rasbora tends to be loosely shoaling rather than tightly schooling — groups of 6 or more move together in a relaxed manner but do not form the tight formations typical of danios or smaller rasboras. The species is seen less often in the trade than its appearance might suggest; most specimens are wild-collected, which can make import stress and acclimation more challenging than with captive-bred fish. Newly imported fish should be quarantined and acclimated gradually to stable blackwater conditions before introduction to the display tank.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is rarely achieved and is generally considered difficult. Like most rasboras, the Clown Rasbora is an egg-scatterer, broadcasting adhesive eggs among fine-leaved plants. Successful spawning attempts typically involve conditioning pairs on live foods, using a separate spawning tank with dense planting such as java moss, hornwort, or spawning mops, and maintaining very soft, acidic water (pH 4.5–5.5, near-zero hardness). Adults will consume eggs if left in the spawning tank and should be removed promptly after spawning. Fry are very small at hatching and require infusoria or commercial fry food initially, graduating to baby brine shrimp as they grow. Documented captive breeding successes are uncommon.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Rasbora kalochroma
primary.notes: Primary source. Species page provides native range (Sumatra, Borneo, Peninsular Malaysia), water parameters (pH 4.0–6.5, temperature 23–28°C, hardness < 5 dGH), adult size (to 10 cm), diet (invertebrates, insects, zooplankton), habitat description (blackwater peat swamps), and husbandry notes including group size and tank setup requirements.

additional.name: FishBase — Rasbora kalochroma
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 10 cm TL), native range (Malaysia, Indonesia, Brunei), taxonomy (Cyprinidae, Cypriniformes), temperature range (23–27°C), and habitat type. Confirms demersal/pelagic habitat in freshwater blackwater environments.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Rasbora Care Guide
additional.notes: General rasbora care overview referenced for feeding guidance, group size recommendations, and compatible tankmates. Clown Rasbora not profiled individually but general rasbora husbandry principles applied.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Rasbora kalochroma species profile
additional.notes: Trade availability context and notes on wild-collected status; confirms soft-acidic blackwater requirement, larger size compared to other rasboras (up to 10 cm), and characteristically loose shoaling behaviour. Recommended minimum group of 6.

additional.name: FishBase — Rasbora kalochroma distribution and ecology
additional.notes: Distribution and ecology data cross-reference confirming Indonesian records (Sumatra, Kalimantan) and Malaysian records (Peninsular Malaysia, Sarawak). Used to populate nativeRange.countries.

fish.breedingNotes: The Clown Rasbora is an egg-scatterer; captive breeding is rarely achieved. Successful attempts require very soft, acidic water (pH 4.5–5.5, near-zero hardness), live food conditioning of the breeding pair, and a fine-leaved spawning medium such as java moss or a spawning mop. Adults will predate eggs and should be removed after spawning. Fry are minute at hatching and require infusoria or a similar first food before graduating to baby brine shrimp.
